justification: Science self-efficacy is inherently multi-dimensional, encompassing perceived ability to understand, evaluate, and engage with scientific information, so no single item captures it fully. The questions span two scale families (0–10 for p16/p17 and 5–7-point Likert for the rest), making simple summation inappropriate; PCA extracts the dominant latent dimension while accommodating scale heterogeneity. Binning the first principal component into 5 quantiles yields an ordinal variable within the ≤15 category constraint while maximising variance explained across all six indicators.

Institutional trust in science is a latent construct that spans trust in scientists, scientific institutions, and science-based policy bodies, requiring multiple items to cover its breadth reliably. The scale mix (one 0–10 item p31_1 alongside 6- and 7-point Likert items) makes raw summation inadvisable, whereas PCA on standardised items recovers the shared trust dimension without distortion from differing response ranges. The first principal component, binned into 5 quantiles, provides a high-importance, high-entropy composite that is well-suited to the bridge regression.

Science and technology engagement covers behavioural frequency (media consumption, event attendance, online activity) across diverse channels, so a single item would miss most of the construct's scope. The items use heterogeneous scales (5- to 13-point), making PCA on standardised scores the most principled aggregation method to extract the common engagement factor. Quantile-binning the first PC into 5 ordered categories keeps the variable within bridge model constraints while preserving the ordinal gradient from low to high engagement.

Information capital reflects access to and use of diverse information sources; the nine 4-point items (p3_1–p3_15, p4, p5) are on an identical scale and function as a checklist of media/source access, making simple summation both theoretically appropriate and psychometrically sound. The single 7-point item p6 adds complementary depth (e.g., frequency or breadth of use) and can be recoded to a 4-point scale before summing without distorting the composite. Binning the total sum into ≤5 ordered categories produces a parsimonious ordinal variable that captures cumulative information resource richness for the bridge model.

Science and technology policy attitudes span support for public funding, regulation, risk perception, and trust in expert-led policy, making this a genuinely multi-faceted construct that no single item can represent adequately. The items use highly heterogeneous scales (4- to 14-point), so PCA on standardised scores is the appropriate method to isolate the dominant attitudinal dimension without scale-range artefacts. Binning the first PC into 5 quantiles yields an ordinal bridge variable that captures the pro- to anti-science-policy gradient while respecting the ≤15 category constraint.
